reviewed_items.reviewer_notes: Passed all gates. The specification (SG-004, INV-002) explicitly requires 'chain_id must match the target chain for replay protection' per outputs/graphs/W3B12_1770272943/osaka-fork-types/SG-004_Authorization_construction.mmd. Reth's transaction pool validator (crates/transaction-pool/src/validate/eth.rs:434-449) only validates that authorization_list is non-empty but does NOT validate individual authorization.chain_id fields against the current chain_id. Transaction's chain_id is validated (L425-431) but not the chain_ids within each authorization tuple. Phase 03 correctly identified that validation may exist in downstream revm execution layer, but this is unverified. Per 01e entry PROP-ff7df16a-inv-020 in outputs/01e_PARTIAL_W4B5_1771748579.json: 'EIP-7702 authorization must not be replayable across different chains' with assertion 'forall auth, chain1, chain2: chain1 != chain2 => !valid_on(auth, chain2) if valid_on(auth, chain1) && auth.chain_id != 0'. Attack path: Attacker creates authorization on Chain A, then submits EIP-7702 transaction on Chain B with chain_id=B but containing authorization with chain_id=A; pool validation accepts it. Impact depends on whether revm validates: if revm validates, this is a pool pollution DoS vector; if revm doesn't validate, this is a critical cross-chain replay vulnerability. Severity: High based on potential for cross-chain authorization replay affecting >33% of network if multiple implementations miss this check. Entry point: P2P (untrusted).
reviewed_items.spec_reference: EIP-7702 authorization must not be replayable across different chains. forall auth, chain1, chain2: chain1 != chain2 => !valid_on(auth, chain2) if valid_on(auth, chain1) && auth.chain_id != 0
